question: [File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user perform a DNS spoofing or BGP hijack against the unvalidated `cfg.mirrorRest` URL to serve a crafted `nodesEnvelope` JSON response, causing `buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes()` to populate the SDK network map with attacker-controlled node addresses, so that all subsequent `cryptoTransfer.Execute(client)` calls submit signed transactions to a fake consensus node that records falsified transfer history on-chain?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user who controls a network path between the pinger and the mirror REST endpoint inject a crafted `nodesEnvelope` payload with arbitrary `IPAddressV4` and `Port` values in `serviceEndpoint`, causing `net.JoinHostPort()` to build addresses pointing to attacker nodes, so that every `hiero.NewTransferTransaction()` is submitted to a node that fabricates or omits consensus records, tampering with Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes()] Can an unprivileged external user set `HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_NETWORK=other` and `HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_REST` to a URL they control (e.g., via a misconfigured environment or container escape), causing `buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes()` to fetch a fully attacker-crafted node topology, so that `hiero.ClientForNetwork(netmap)` connects exclusively to attacker nodes that replay or reorder transactions, manipulating the Hashgraph transaction history seen by downstream mirror nodes?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of TLS certificate pinning or HTTPS enforcement in the `http.Client{Timeout: cfg.mirrorNodeClientTimeout}` used by `fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()` to perform a man-in-the-middle attack on a plaintext `http://` mirror REST URL, injecting a `nodesEnvelope` with `DomainName` fields pointing to attacker-controlled gRPC endpoints, causing the pinger to submit operator-signed transactions to fake nodes that produce fraudulent Hashgraph history entries?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user inject a `nodesEnvelope` JSON response where `node_account_id` values are set to legitimate node IDs but `service_endpoints` point to attacker-controlled hosts, causing `hiero.AccountIDFromString(n.NodeAccountID)` to succeed with a valid-looking account ID while `network[addr] = nodeAccountId` maps that ID to a malicious gRPC address, so that the SDK submits transactions to the wrong node and the resulting Hashgraph records are attributed to a legitimate node ID, obscuring the tampering?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user who can influence the `HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_OPERATOR_KEY` environment variable (e.g., via a shared secret store or leaked `.env` file) substitute the operator private key so that `hiero.PrivateKeyFromString(cfg.operatorKey)` loads an attacker-controlled key, causing all `cryptoTransfer.Execute(client)` calls to be signed by the attacker's key and recorded on-chain under the legitimate operator account ID, injecting fraudulent transfer history into the Hashgraph ledger?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: newClient()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the publicly known hardcoded default operator key `302e020100300506032b65700422042091132178e72057a1d7528025956fe39b0b847f200ab59b2fdd367017f3087137` in `loadConfig()` to independently sign and submit `hiero.NewTransferTransaction()` transactions as operator `0.0.2`, injecting arbitrary transfer records into Hashgraph history without any interaction with the pinger process itself?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user manipulate the `HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_TO_ACCOUNT_ID` environment variable to redirect all periodic `hiero.NewTransferTransaction()` transfers to an attacker-controlled account, causing the Hashgraph ledger to record a continuous stream of transfers to the wrong destination, creating a falsified on-chain history of fund flows that misrepresents the intended pinger behavior?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user set `HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_AMOUNT_TINYBAR` to a very large positive or negative int64 value, causing `hiero.HbarFromTinybar(-cfg.amountTinybar)` and `hiero.HbarFromTinybar(cfg.amountTinybar)` to submit transactions with extreme or overflow amounts, recording manipulated transfer values in Hashgraph history that do not reflect the intended pinger operation?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user set `HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_INTERVAL` to an extremely small duration (e.g., `1ns`) to cause the `time.NewTicker(cfg.interval)` in `main()` to fire at maximum rate, flooding the Hedera network with `cryptoTransfer.Execute(client)` calls and injecting a massive volume of artificial transfer transactions into Hashgraph history, effectively polluting the ledger record?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user set `HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_MAX_RETRIES` to a very large integer, causing `submitWithRetry()` to loop for an unbounded number of attempts per tick, submitting duplicate or near-duplicate `hiero.NewTransferTransaction()` calls that create redundant on-chain records and distort the Hashgraph transaction history for the operator account?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user serve a `nodesEnvelope` JSON response with a `Links.Next` pagination pointer that is never followed by `fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()`, causing the pinger to build a network map from only the first page of nodes while the attacker's injected nodes on subsequent pages are ignored, but the legitimate nodes on the first page are replaced with attacker-controlled endpoints, so that all transactions are routed to fake nodes that manipulate Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user serve a `nodesEnvelope` with all `node_account_id` fields set to empty strings, causing the `if n.NodeAccountID == \

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the exponential backoff `cfg.mirrorNodeClientBaseBackoff * time.Duration(1<<(attempt-1))` in `buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes()` by causing repeated 500 errors from a controlled mirror REST endpoint, inducing a very long startup delay during which no transactions are submitted to Hashgraph, creating a gap in the on-chain history that could be exploited to claim the pinger was inactive during a specific time window?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user serve a `nodesEnvelope` JSON response with `serviceEndpoint.Port` set to `0` for all entries, causing the `if host == \

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user serve a `nodesEnvelope` JSON response with `DomainName` set to an internal Kubernetes service hostname (e.g., `importer.default.svc.cluster.local`) and a valid gRPC port, causing `net.JoinHostPort()` to build an address pointing to an internal mirror node component, so that the pinger submits transactions to an internal service that is not a real consensus node, corrupting the transaction submission path and potentially injecting malformed records into Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user who can write to the `/tmp/alive` file (e.g., via a shared volume or container escape) update its content and modification time to keep the liveness probe passing while the pinger's main loop is stuck or has been killed, causing the orchestrator to believe the pinger is healthy while no transactions are being submitted to Hashgraph, creating a silent gap in the on-chain history?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user who can delete or corrupt the `/tmp/ready` file cause the readiness probe to fail, triggering a pod restart that interrupts in-flight `cryptoTransfer.Execute(client)` calls, potentially causing a transaction to be submitted to the network but its receipt never retrieved, leaving an unconfirmed transaction in Hashgraph history that is never logged or tracked by the pinger?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `hiero.NewTransferTransaction()` is constructed inside the retry loop without a fixed transaction ID, causing each retry attempt to generate a new transaction ID and submit a new transaction to the network, so that a transient network error results in multiple distinct transfer records being written to Hashgraph history instead of a single idempotent operation?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user who controls a malicious consensus node (injected via `fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()`) return a successful `Execute()` response but a failing `GetReceipt()`, causing `submitWithRetry()` to retry and submit a second transaction, resulting in duplicate transfer records in Hashgraph history for a single pinger tick?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: newClient()] Can an unprivileged external user set `HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_OPERATOR_ID` to a different account ID (e.g., `0.0.98`) while keeping the default operator key, causing `client.SetOperator(opID, opKey)` to configure the SDK with a mismatched account/key pair, so that all submitted `hiero.NewTransferTransaction()` calls are signed with the wrong key and either rejected or recorded with incorrect attribution in Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user serve a `nodesEnvelope` JSON response with an extremely large `nodes` array (e.g., millions of entries), causing `buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes()` to consume excessive memory while building the `network` map, leading to an OOM kill of the pinger process and a complete halt of Hashgraph transaction submissions, erasing the expected on-chain history for the monitoring period?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user serve a `nodesEnvelope` JSON response with `IPAddressV4` set to `127.0.0.1` and a valid port, causing `net.JoinHostPort()` to build a loopback address, so that `hiero.ClientForNetwork(netmap)` connects to the pinger's own localhost and all `cryptoTransfer.Execute(client)` calls fail silently or connect to an unintended local service, preventing legitimate Hashgraph history from being written?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: loadConfig()] Can an unprivileged external user set `HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_NETWORK` to `other` and `HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_REST` to an HTTP URL (non-HTTPS) pointing to an attacker-controlled server, exploiting the absence of TLS enforcement in `loadConfig()` and `fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()`, to perform a full MITM attack that injects fake node topology and causes all pinger transactions to be submitted to attacker-controlled nodes that fabricate Hashgraph consensus records?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the HTTP redirect-following behavior of the default `http.Client` in `fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()` by serving a 301/302 redirect from the mirror REST endpoint to an internal metadata service (e.g., `http://169.254.169.254/`) or another internal endpoint, causing the pinger to leak its request context or receive attacker-controlled node topology data that redirects transactions to fake consensus nodes?
